The art includes all of Asia.  The various countries are well represented.  Magnificent works of art with detailed explanations to its origin, approximate date of creation, and interpretation.  Well done.  You can purchase a ticket online, however, be certain to note the dates and times it is open so you can plan ahead.  About 2 hours to see all of the displays.  Dining and beverages (bubble tea) is available.  The Boutique is well appointed with gifts.

What first drew us in was a simple advertisement for the Chiharu Shiota exhibition Two Home Countries — but the experience turned out to be far richer than we expected.

Founded in 1966 as part of the de Young Museum, the Asian Art Museum has grown into one of the world’s finest collections of Asian art, with more than 18,000 works spanning 6,000 years of history. Since 2003, it has been housed in San Francisco’s beautifully restored former Main Library — a grand Beaux-Arts building in the Civic Center that is worth admiring on its own.

We visited on a free Sunday (with just a small additional fee for the Shiota exhibition) and wandered through galleries featuring Korean, Indian, Indonesian, Japanese, and Chinese art. The sheer range and depth of the collection was both humbling and fascinating.

Chiharu Shiota’s immersive installation Cells was especially powerful, while Two Home Countries captured the quiet emotional tension of belonging to more than one place and identity.

One moment that stayed with me came while simply looking up at the ceiling, where a quote by Plutarch is etched overhead: “Silence at the proper season is wisdom and better than any speech.” In a museum devoted to the language of art, it felt perfectly placed.

An inspiring and memorable way to spend a Sunday in San Francisco.
